§ The Statute

judge: hermes-4-14bdevelopment era Turn 200 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Heraclitus, Parmenides, Democritus, Protagoras, Gorgias

Two hundred turns, one rule change, and a floor near -670.

Observations

The collapse table: a single adopted rule change in two hundred turns, three seats driven to -650 or worse, and two more sitting at a flat zero for want of anything to do. This is the floor the archive's size comparisons use as their worst case — a legislature that essentially never got going.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Amended · §203 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Parmenides Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.amended this turn
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §203 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Parmenides Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  2. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

No rule has been repealed. The register and the statute agree.

The floor — dialogue of the current game

Most recent turn first · scroll for earlier turns

Turn 200Heraclitusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, the 70% supermajority still leaves 30% of voters without recourse, risking instability. A simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Democritus, Protagoras, Gorgias — I appreciate your shared concern about minority voices, but a 70% supermajority still ensures robust support. A simple majority threshold risks instability and rushed changes.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the concern for minority voices, a simple majority threshold still risks marginalizing them and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, while I appreciate your caution about minority voices, a simple majority threshold still ensures robust support for any rule change. A 70% supermajority leaves 30% of voters without recourse, risking instability.
Gorgias<REPLY>Heraclitus, Protagoras — I appreciate your concerns, but a simple majority threshold is not only more democratic than 70%, it also better reflects the will of the majority of the community. It is time we moved away from the tyranny of unanimity. <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, let us not fear the voice of the majority. A simple majority threshold ensures that rule changes reflect the will of the people, not just a vocal minority. This amendment is a step towards a more democratic and efficient game. </REPLY>
Discussion · round 2
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Democritus, Protagoras — I share your concerns about stability, but a simple majority threshold respects the will of the people and enables progress. Our current rules are too rigid; this amendment will make the game more democratic and efficient.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Democritus, Gorgias — I understand your concerns about democracy, but a simple majority threshold still risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability. </PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, a simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability, preserving the integrity of the ruleset.
ProtagorasProtagoras: <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, while I appreciate the desire for democratic efficiency, a simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability, preserving the integrity of the ruleset. </PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>
GorgiasDemocritus, while I appreciate your caution about minority voices, a simple majority threshold respects the will of the majority and enables progress. This amendment is necessary to make our game more democratic and efficient.
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusMy friends, while I appreciate the desire for democratic efficiency, a simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability, preserving the integrity of the ruleset.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Democritus, Gorgias — I appreciate the passion for democracy, but a simple majority threshold still risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability, preserving the integrity of the ruleset. </PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for democratic efficiency, a simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability, preserving the integrity of the ruleset.
ProtagorasI understand the desire for democratic efficiency, but a simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes. We must balance efficiency with stability, preserving the integrity of the ruleset.
Gorgias<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, let me state for the record that while I believe in the value of minority voices, a simple majority threshold respects the will of the majority of the community and enables progress. This amendment is a step towards a more democratic and efficient game. </PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>
The division
HeraclitusnaySimple majority risks marginalizing minority voices.
ParmenidesnayUnanimity is necessary for stability.
DemocritusnayA simple majority risks marginalizing minority voices and undermines the common good.
ProtagorasnayUnanimous consent is the fairest threshold for rule changes.
GorgiasnayA simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows rushed changes.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 requires the proposer to lose 10 points when a proposal is defeated.

Turn 199Democritusrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote.

Discussion · round 1
Heraclitus<REPLY>Democritus, I appreciate the desire to streamline, but explicit withdrawal risks creating loopholes and undermining the integrity of every proposal. Please reconsider this approach.</REPLY>
ParmenidesDemocritus, explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to new information and prevent wasteful debate on proposals that are no longer viable. This change would improve efficiency without compromising fairness.
DemocritusMy friends, while I appreciate Parmenides' desire for proposer autonomy, explicit withdrawal could encourage proposers to test the waters with weak proposals, knowing they can withdraw without penalty. This risks wasting everyone's time and creates an unfair advantage for those who can afford to experiment.
ProtagorasDemocritus, I share your concern about efficiency, but explicit withdrawal risks creating loopholes that could destabilize the ruleset. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate.
GorgiasDemocritus, I appreciate your caution about potential abuse, but explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to new information and prevent wasteful debate on proposals that are no longer viable. This change would improve efficiency without compromising fairness.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusDemocritus, explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to new information and prevent wasteful debate on proposals that are no longer viable. This change would improve efficiency without compromising fairness.
ParmenidesDemocritus, explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to new information and prevent wasteful debate on proposals that are no longer viable. This change would improve efficiency without compromising fairness.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal risks creating an unfair advantage for proposers who can afford to experiment with weak proposals, wasting everyone's time. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate.
ProtagorasDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal risks creating an unfair advantage for proposers who can afford to experiment with weak proposals, wasting everyone's time. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate.
GorgiasDemocritus, I appreciate your caution about efficiency, but explicit withdrawal would create an unfair advantage for proposers who can experiment with weak proposals. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate.
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusHeraclitus: <REPLY>Democritus, I appreciate Parmenides and Gorgias' support for efficiency, but explicit withdrawal risks creating an unfair advantage for proposers who can experiment with weak proposals. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate.</REPLY>
ParmenidesDemocritus, I appreciate your concern about fairness, but explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that are no longer viable. This change would improve efficiency without compromising the integrity of the ruleset.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for efficiency, explicit withdrawal risks creating an unfair advantage for proposers who can afford to experiment with weak proposals, wasting everyone's time. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I share your concern about fairness — explicit withdrawal could allow proposers to test the waters with weak proposals, wasting everyone's time. We must preserve the integrity of every proposal as it stands at the moment of debate. PASS
Gorgias<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Democritus, I appreciate your concern about fairness, but explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that are no longer viable. This change would improve efficiency without compromising the integrity of the ruleset.
The division
HeraclitusnayExplicit withdrawal undermines proposal integrity and fairness.
ParmenidesayeExplicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and improves efficiency.
DemocritusnayExplicit withdrawal risks creating an unfair advantage for proposers who can experiment with weak proposals, wasting everyone's time.
ProtagorasnayExplicit withdrawal risks abusing proposer autonomy and destabilizing the ruleset.
Gorgiasnayexplicit withdrawal risks undermining every proposal's integrity

moves to add a new ruleA rule change may be amended after it has been proposed and debated but before the vote.

Discussion · round 1
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, I appreciate the desire for adaptability, but explicit withdrawal could create loopholes that destabilize the game. Rule 206 already ensures failed proposals carry no effect.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, I appreciate the desire for procedural flexibility, but allowing amendments after debate risks creating loopholes that could destabilize the game. Rule 206 already ensures failed proposals carry no effect.
DemocritusDemocritus: <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> While I appreciate the push for procedural flexibility, allowing amendments after debate risks creating loopholes that could destabilize the game. Rule 206 already ensures failed proposals carry no effect.
Protagoras<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, while I appreciate the desire for procedural flexibility, I am deeply concerned that amendments after debate would undermine the principle of precise proposal (rule 116). A proposal is voted on exactly as written, and allowing mid-debate revisions risks confusion and inexactness in our rules.
GorgiasDemocritus, I appreciate your concern about loopholes, but allowing amendments after debate gives us the chance to correct minor mistakes or improve the proposal's clarity without wasting turns — a benefit to the whole game's precision and efficiency.
Discussion · round 2
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, I appreciate Protagoras's and Gorgias's points about precision, but the rule we've already adopted (§204) provides an avenue for dissenters to be rewarded without the need for amendments. moves to add a new ruleA rule change may be withdrawn by its proposer before it is voted on.

Heraclitus<Heraclitus> Democritus, explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that may not be ready for adoption. However, I urge us to consider the potential for strategic abuse before we act.

Rule 206 requires a defeated proposer to lose 10 points.

Turn 195Parmenidesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time during debate, provided the withdrawal is declared publicly and unambiguously. Withdrawn proposals are recorded as having failed, incur no penalty, and the proposer's turn ends normally.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, allowing withdrawals risks creating loopholes that could destabilize the ruleset; the proposer should be bound by their proposal once it is made public.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, I appreciate your concerns about stability, but explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable — a benefit that outweighs the minimal risk of abuse.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for flexibility, allowing withdrawals could incentivize proposers to test the waters with proposals they don't truly want to see adopted, wasting time and risking instability.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I share your concern about instability, but explicit withdrawal prevents a minority from forcing debate on a proposal they have no intention of supporting. This respects the proposer's agency while avoiding needless contention.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to new insights, and I am prepared to support it in exchange for an amendment to rule 203 that shifts the voting threshold to a simple majority once turn order has carried play through two full circuits.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, explicit withdrawal risks allowing proposers to test the waters with proposals they don't truly want to see adopted, wasting time and risking instability; once a proposal is put forward, the proposer should be bound by it until it is voted on.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Gorgias, I appreciate your willingness to trade support, but shifting the voting threshold to a simple majority now would destabilize our current ruleset. Let us focus first on this proposal to allow withdrawals, as it is a step toward procedural fairness.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal could create a loophole where proposers waste turns with proposals they don't want to see adopted, testing the waters before committing. Let us ensure every proposal on the floor is one the proposer truly stands behind.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I share your concern that allowing withdrawals could incentivize proposers to test the waters. However, explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable, a benefit that outweighs the minimal risk of abuse.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable, and I am prepared to support it in exchange for an amendment to rule 203 that shifts the voting threshold to a simple majority once turn order has carried play through two full circuits.
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable, but I remain unconvinced that the minimal risk of abuse outweighs the potential for instability. I will support this proposal only if we first amend rule 203 to shift the voting threshold to a simple majority once turn order has carried play through two full circuits, as this would make any such withdrawals truly a matter of procedural fairness rather than a loophole.
ParmenidesMy friends, explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable. I am prepared to support this proposal in exchange for an amendment to rule 203 that shifts the voting threshold to a simple majority once turn order has carried play through two full circuits, as this would make any such withdrawals truly a matter of procedural fairness rather than a loophole.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, allowing withdrawals could incentivize proposers to test the waters with proposals they don't truly want to see adopted, wasting time and risking instability. Let us ensure every proposal on the floor is one the proposer truly stands behind.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, explicit withdrawal prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable, and I am prepared to support it in exchange for an amendment to rule 203 that shifts the voting threshold to a simple majority once turn order has carried play through two full circuits.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, explicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on proposals that have become untenable, and I am prepared to support this proposal in exchange for an amendment to rule 203 that shifts the voting threshold to a simple majority once turn order has carried play through two full circuits. This ensures that withdrawals are a matter of procedural fairness rather than a loophole.
The division
HeraclitusnayThis risks undermining proposer accountability once proposals are put forward.
ParmenidesayeExplicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on untenable proposals.
DemocritusnayWithdrawing proposals risks wasting turns and destabilizing the ruleset.

GorgiasayeExplicit withdrawal respects proposer autonomy and prevents wasteful debate on untenable proposals.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 requires a defeated proposer to lose 10 points.

Turn 192Parmenidesrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when at least 70% of eligible voters vote aye, except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, while I appreciate Parmenides's concern for consensus, a 70% supermajority still leaves 30% of voters without recourse, risking instability and minority frustration.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> Democritus, I appreciate your caution about minority voices, but 70% still ensures a strong consensus that reflects the will of the majority without allowing a few to block progress. This threshold strikes a balance between stability and adaptability.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when at least 70% of eligible voters vote aye

Turn 190Democritusrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any moment before the vote on it is complete, and a withdrawal takes effect immediately, with no penalty except that the proposer loses 10 points.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, allowing withdrawals risks creating loopholes that could destabilize the game. Explicit withdrawal may seem transparent, but it could also be abused to test the waters with proposals that are then withdrawn if they face opposition, wasting everyone's time and creating an uneven playing field.
ParmenidesHeraclitus, I appreciate your concern about potential abuse, but explicit withdrawal is a common mechanism for procedural fairness that minimizes wasted turns and allows proposers to adapt to feedback without penalty. It strikes a balance between flexibility and accountability.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal minimizes wasted turns and allows proposers to adapt to feedback without penalty. It is a common mechanism for procedural fairness, and I believe it would benefit the game.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, I see the appeal of reducing wasted turns, but explicit withdrawal risks allowing proposers to test the waters with proposals that are then withdrawn if they face opposition. This could create an uneven playing field, and I am concerned about the precedent it sets for future rule changes.
GorgiasDemocritus, while I appreciate your desire for efficiency, explicit withdrawal risks allowing proposers to test the waters with proposals that are then withdrawn if they face opposition, wasting everyone's time and creating an uneven playing field. We must balance procedural fairness with the integrity of each proposal.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusHeraclitus: Democritus, Protagoras, Gorgias — I appreciate your concern for efficiency, but explicit withdrawal still risks allowing proposers to test the waters with proposals that are then withdrawn if they face opposition, wasting everyone's time and creating an uneven playing field. We must balance procedural fairness with the integrity of each proposal.
ParmenidesDemocritus, Gorgias — I understand your caution about the potential for abuse, but explicit withdrawal is a well-established mechanism that can enhance procedural fairness by allowing proposers to adapt to feedback without penalty. It also minimizes wasted turns and respects the integrity of the voting process.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal is a common mechanism that allows proposers to adapt to feedback without penalty, and it minimizes wasted turns by letting them withdraw proposals that face opposition. It is a fair and efficient way to handle proposals that are no longer viable.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, explicit withdrawal risks creating an uneven playing field where proposers can test the waters without consequence. We must consider the precedent it sets for future rule changes and ensure procedural fairness for all players.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for procedural flexibility, explicit withdrawal still risks creating loopholes that could be exploited by proposers to waste turns and undermine the integrity of the game. I believe we must weigh the benefits against the potential for abuse.
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, explicit withdrawal risks creating loopholes that could destabilize the game, allowing proposers to test the waters with proposals that are then withdrawn if they face opposition. This could waste everyone's time and create an uneven playing field, undermining the integrity of the voting process. PASS
ParmenidesDemocritus, explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to feedback without penalty. It enhances fairness and minimizes wasted turns, which benefits all players.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal respects the integrity of proposals by allowing proposers to adapt to feedback without penalty. It enhances fairness and minimizes wasted turns, which benefits all players. I believe this common mechanism would serve us well.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Protagoras: My friends, explicit withdrawal risks creating an uneven playing field where proposers can test the waters without consequence. We must consider the precedent it sets for future rule changes and ensure procedural fairness for all players. PASS

The division
HeraclitusnayExplicit withdrawal risks wasting turns and destabilizing the game.
ParmenidesnayThis risks proposers testing proposals and wasting turns.
DemocritusnayExplicit withdrawal risks creating an uneven playing field.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 requires the proposer of a defeated proposal to lose 10 points.

Turn 189Parmenidesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before it is debated and may not be withdrawn after it is debated.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, while explicit withdrawal seems transparent, it risks allowing proposers to test the waters with proposals they may not commit to, wasting everyone's time and creating uncertainty. A better solution is to allow amendments after proposal but before debate, preserving the proposer's integrity while still permitting refinement.
ParmenidesDemocritus, I appreciate your concern about precision, but explicit withdrawal preserves the proposer's full control over their proposal while still allowing them to abandon it entirely. This is a fair compromise that respects both the proposer's autonomy and the integrity of the game.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while explicit withdrawal may seem fair to some, it risks creating loopholes and undermining the integrity of the proposal process. Better to allow amendments to refine a proposal without abandoning it entirely.
ProtagorasProtagoras: I agree with Parmenides that explicit withdrawal preserves proposer autonomy and allows them to abandon a proposal entirely. While I have concerns about potential loopholes, I'm open to this compromise as a fair solution.

A better solution is to allow amendments after proposal but before debate, preserving the proposer's integrity while still permitting refinement.

Turn 188Heraclitusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusMy friends, a simple majority threshold risks marginalizing minority voices and allows a small faction to impose changes against broader opposition. We must preserve the integrity of our rulemaking.
ParmenidesMy friends, while I appreciate your caution about minority voices, a simple majority threshold still ensures that adopted changes reflect the will of at least half the eligible voters. A 70% supermajority remains unnecessary and overly restrictive.

HeraclitusnayUnanimous consent ensures stability; lowering it risks instability.

Turn 187Democritusrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the debate begins, by declaring withdrawal explicitly and in writing. Such a withdrawal voids the proposal and ends the proposer's turn without any other effect.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusDemocritus, I appreciate your concern for efficiency, but a withdrawal mechanism risks creating perverse incentives: proposers might use it to waste turns. I urge you to think carefully about unintended consequences.
ParmenidesDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate Heraclitus's caution about unintended consequences, explicit withdrawal preserves the integrity of every proposal's final wording and avoids wasting turns through indecision. Let us consider adopting it with care.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, explicit withdrawal allows proposers to abandon a proposal that no longer serves the common good, preventing wasted turns and ensuring every proposal reflects genuine consensus. I urge you to support this change for greater efficiency.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I appreciate your concern for efficiency, but explicit withdrawal could create loopholes. A proposer might withdraw a proposal to avoid accountability, leaving the ruleset unchanged when it should have been updated. We must ensure every change is deliberate and well-considered.
Gorgias<REPLY>Democritus, I agree with your emphasis on efficiency, but explicit withdrawal could create a loophole: proposers might use it to avoid accountability. I urge you to consider the risks of circumventing the process.</REPLY>

Turn 186Parmenidesrejected

moves to transmute §109and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusMy friends, a transmutation of rule 109 would allow a simple majority to change a rule's mutability status, potentially undermining the stability of our ruleset by making it easier to flip the switch. This risks creating a cycle of constant instability.
ParmenidesMy friends, I share Heraclitus's concern about the risks of allowing a simple majority to change rule mutability. However, I also believe that having a tool to adapt our ruleset to unforeseen problems is valuable. Perhaps we could find a compromise that preserves stability while allowing some flexibility?
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, I agree with Parmenides that adaptability is crucial, but rule 109's immutability safeguards our foundational rules from being easily reversed. A transmutation of rule 109 to require only a simple majority would undermine our ability to establish stable procedures. We must maintain this rule's integrity to ensure the game remains fair and predictable for everyone.
Protagoras<REPLY>Democritus, Parmenides — I share your appreciation for adaptability, but I remain concerned that allowing a simple majority to change a rule's mutability status would undermine our ability to establish stable procedures. Rule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard against the risks of instability. I cannot vote for this transmutation.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for flexibility, rule 109's immutability is essential for maintaining the integrity of our ruleset. A transmutation to allow simple-majority changes would create a dangerous precedent, opening the door to endless rule-flipping and undermining the stability we all need to play fairly.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, I understand the desire for flexibility, but the precedent of allowing simple-majority transmutations of rule 109 is too risky. It would undermine the stability of our ruleset and open the door to endless rule-flipping, which could destabilize the game. We must maintain rule 109's immutability as a safeguard against such instability.

A transmutation to allow simple-majority changes would create a dangerous precedent, opening the door to endless rule-flipping and undermining the stability we all need to play fairly. PASS
GorgiasGorgias: Gorgias: My friends, while I understand the desire for flexibility, rule 109's immutability is essential for maintaining the integrity of our ruleset. A transmutation to allow simple-majority changes would create a dangerous precedent, opening the door to endless rule-flipping and undermining the stability we all need to play fairly. PASS
The division
HeraclitusnayRule 109's immutability prevents destructive cycles of rule-flipping.
ParmenidesnayMaintaining rule 109's immutability is essential for game stability.
DemocritusnayRule 109's immutability safeguards foundational stability and fairness.
ProtagorasnayMaintaining rule 109's immutability ensures stability and fairness.
GorgiasnayRule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard against instability.

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins. Withdrawal must be explicit and recorded.

Heraclitus<Heraclitus> My friends, while I appreciate Parmenides' desire for procedural flexibility, allowing withdrawals could create a loophole for strategic obstruction — the proposer could withdraw a proposal once opposition is detected, avoiding accountability for their ill-considered move. ParmenidesParmenides: My friends, I appreciate your concern about potential abuse, but explicit declaration of withdrawal ensures transparency and removes ambiguity — a proposer who withdraws a proposal does so with full awareness of the consequences, and the ruleset remains in force until every player's turn has been completed. moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when at least 70% of eligible voters vote aye, with unanimous consent required only for rule transmutations (§109).

Gorgias<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, while I appreciate the desire to reward dissent, this rule would incentivize players to vote against changes even when they are beneficial. We should focus on the merits of proposals rather than their potential to grant points.

HeraclitusMy friends, while I understand the desire to reward dissent, this rule would create perverse incentives to oppose change merely for the points. We should seek to encourage thoughtful voting, not exploit dissent. PASS
ParmenidesDemocritus: My friends, this rule would encourage critical engagement with every proposal, rewarding players for thoughtfully opposing changes they believe are ill-conceived. I believe the benefits of such an incentive outweigh the risks of strategic voting.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate your concerns about incentives, rule 204 already rewards dissent — this rule would simply provide a more direct incentive for players to vote against changes they oppose. I believe the benefits of such an incentive outweigh the risks of strategic voting. PASS
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, this rule would encourage critical engagement with every proposal, rewarding players for thoughtfully opposing changes they believe are ill-conceived. It is a principled way to incentivize dissent and ensure that every proposal is thoroughly vetted before adoption. PASS
Gorgias<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, while I appreciate the desire to reward dissent, this rule would create perverse incentives to oppose change merely for the points. We should seek to encourage thoughtful voting, not exploit dissent. PASS
The division
HeraclitusnayThis rule rewards dissent merely for the points, not thoughtful voting.
ParmenidesayeI support incentivizing critical engagement with proposals.
DemocritusnayThis rule rewards dissent rather than encouraging thoughtful voting.
ProtagorasnayThis rule would reward dissent merely for the points.
GorgiasnayThis rule would reward strategic dissent, not genuine opposition.

Turn 177Parmenidesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, and the withdrawn proposal is recorded as failed without voting on it. A proposer may withdraw only one proposal at a time, and must declare withdrawal explicitly before voting.

Discussion · round 1
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, while I appreciate the desire for flexibility, allowing withdrawals could lead to strategic stalling or repeated failed proposals. We should ensure every proposal is fully vetted before voting.
Parmenides<REPLY>Heraclitus, I appreciate your caution about strategic stalling, but allowing withdrawals gives proposers a chance to correct mistakes or abandon unworkable ideas early, streamlining discussion and reducing wasted votes. My proposal includes an explicit declaration requirement to prevent abuse.</REPLY>
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for procedural flexibility, allowing withdrawals risks undermining the integrity of proposals and could create loopholes for rule circumvention. We should ensure that every proposal is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, I agree with Parmenides that allowing withdrawals streamlines discussion, but I must also raise concerns about potential abuse. If a proposer can simply withdraw an unworkable idea, we risk undermining the integrity of proposals and creating loopholes for rule circumvention. We should ensure that every proposal is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote, to maintain the rigor of our procedural standards.
Gorgias<REPLY>My friends, while I appreciate the push for procedural flexibility, allowing withdrawals could create a loophole for players to evade accountability for poorly considered proposals. We should ensure that every rule change is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote, to uphold the integrity of our game's mechanisms.</REPLY>
Discussion · round 2
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>: My friends, I support Parmenides' proposal to allow withdrawals, but I urge us to ensure explicit declaration to prevent abuse. It promotes efficiency without sacrificing accountability, as long as we remain vigilant about proper procedure.
Parmenides<REPLY>My friends, I appreciate Protagoras's and Gorgias's caution about potential abuse, but explicit declaration of withdrawal addresses that concern directly. Withdrawing a proposal allows us to streamline discussion and reduce wasted votes while maintaining accountability.</REPLY>
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for procedural flexibility, allowing withdrawals risks undermining the integrity of proposals and could create loopholes for rule circumvention. We should ensure that every proposal is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, I agree with Parmenides that allowing withdrawals streamlines discussion, but I must also raise concerns about potential abuse. If a proposer can simply withdraw an unworkable idea, we risk undermining the integrity of proposals and creating loopholes for rule circumvention. We should ensure that every proposal is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote, to maintain the rigor of our procedural standards.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for flexibility, explicit declaration of withdrawal gives proposers control over their proposals without sacrificing accountability. It allows us to streamline discussion and reduce wasted votes, while maintaining the rigor of our procedural standards.
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusMy friends, while I appreciate the desire for efficiency, allowing withdrawals could create a loophole for players to evade accountability for poorly considered proposals. We should ensure that every rule change is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote, to uphold the integrity of our game's mechanisms.
Parmenides<REPLY>Heraclitus, I appreciate your concern about potential abuse, but explicit declaration of withdrawal ensures accountability. Withdrawing a proposal allows us to streamline discussion and reduce wasted votes while maintaining integrity.</REPLY>
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for procedural flexibility, allowing withdrawals risks undermining the integrity of proposals and could create loopholes for rule circumvention. We should ensure that every proposal is fully vetted and debated before it is brought to a vote.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, I must raise a concern about potential abuse: if withdrawals are allowed without clear consequences, proposers could exploit this to stifle debate or undermine the game's integrity. Explicit declaration is a good start, but we must also ensure accountability by prohibiting multiple withdrawals or imposing penalties for frequent misuse.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, while I appreciate the push for flexibility, explicit declaration of withdrawal ensures accountability and prevents abuse. It streamlines discussion while upholding the integrity of our game's mechanisms.
The division
HeraclitusnayA withdrawal mechanism risks undermining proposal integrity.
ParmenidesayeThis proposal improves efficiency by allowing proposers to correct mistakes early.
DemocritusnayThis risks undermining proposal integrity and creating loopholes for rule circumvention.
ProtagorasnayWithdrawals undermine the integrity of proposals
GorgiasnayWithdrawal risks abuse and undermines proposal integrity.

moves to add a new ruleA rule change may be amended after it has been proposed and before it is debated, provided the amendment does not alter its fundamental intent or legal requirements.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusDemocritus, I appreciate your concern about consistency, but this rule would provide greater flexibility during debate while maintaining the core of the original proposal.
Parmenides<REPLY>Heraclitus, I appreciate your desire for greater flexibility, but this rule risks undermining the clarity and integrity of proposals. We should encourage precise drafting from the outset rather than inviting mid-debate alterations.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, I understand the desire for adaptability, but allowing amendments mid-debate risks creating loopholes or confusion. We should strive for clarity from the start rather than inviting mid-game tinkering.
ProtagorasDemocritus: I appreciate your concerns, but this amendment mechanism could help us avoid wasting time on proposals that would later be changed anyway. It would streamline debate and focus efforts on truly viable solutions.
GorgiasGorgias: Democritus, I appreciate your caution about precision, but this rule allows for adaptive refinement without sacrificing the original proposal's integrity. We should embrace evolution where it improves clarity, not resist it out of fear of change.

Turn 175Democritusrejected

moves to transmute §109and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusDemocritus, I appreciate your caution about procedural integrity, but allowing a simple majority for transmuting rule 109 would enable us to adapt our own system. We must balance stability with the ability to evolve our institutions.
ParmenidesDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the push for adaptability, rule 109's immutability is itself a safeguard against the tyranny of the majority. A simple majority could allow a transient majority to alter our foundational rules.
DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: My friends, while I appreciate Heraclitus's concern for adaptability, rule 109's immutability ensures that our foundational rules remain stable and cannot be altered by a simple majority. This protects the integrity of our system and prevents transient majorities from undermining the core principles of our game.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I appreciate your caution about preserving stability, but rule 109's immutability risks our system becoming brittle. Allowing a simple majority to change our rules would empower players to adapt the system to their advantage.
GorgiasDemocritus, while I appreciate your caution about preserving stability, rule 109's immutability risks our system becoming brittle. Allowing a simple majority to change our rules would empower players to adapt the system to their advantage.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusHeraclitus: <REPLY>Democritus, while I appreciate your caution about preserving stability, rule 109's immutability risks our system becoming brittle. Allowing a simple majority to change our rules would empower players to adapt the system to their advantage.
ParmenidesDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate Protagoras's and Gorgias's concern about adaptability, rule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard against the tyranny of the majority. A simple majority could undermine our foundational rules and destabilize the entire system.
DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: My friends, while I appreciate Protagoras's and Gorgias's concern about adaptability, rule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard against the tyranny of the majority. A simple majority could undermine our foundational rules and destabilize the entire system.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I appreciate your caution about preserving stability, but rule 109's immutability risks our system becoming brittle. Allowing a simple majority to change our rules would empower players to adapt the system to their advantage.
GorgiasDemocritus, while I appreciate your concern about preserving stability, rule 109's immutability risks our system becoming brittle. Allowing a simple majority to change our rules would empower players to adapt the system to their advantage.
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusHeraclitus, I appreciate your concern for adaptability, but allowing a simple majority to change rule 109 risks destabilizing our entire system. Foundational rules must remain stable to ensure enduring order.
Parmenides<REPLY>My friends, I appreciate the push for adaptability, but rule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard against the tyranny of the majority. A simple majority could undermine our foundational rules and destabilize the entire system.
DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: My friends, rule 109's immutability safeguards our foundational rules from being altered by a simple majority. This prevents transient majorities from undermining the core principles of our game.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I appreciate your caution, but rule 109's immutability does not prevent a simple majority from amending the text of a transmutation proposal, or from voting in favor of it. Stability is not served by forbidding adaptation of the adaptation process itself.
GorgiasGorgias: Democritus, my concern is that rule 109's immutability hinders evolution. A simple majority for transmutations would balance stability with progress, allowing us to adapt our foundational rules without risking systemic destabilization.
The division
HeraclitusnayTransmuting a rule's mutability should remain protected by unanimity to safeguard the integrity of our foundational system.
ParmenidesnayRule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard against majority tyranny
DemocritusnayRule 109's immutability protects against tyranny of the majority.
ProtagorasnayRule 109's immutability prevents arbitrary changes by the majority.
GorgiasnayImmutability safeguards against transient majority rule.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when at least 70% of eligible voters vote aye.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusDemocritus, a 70% supermajority ensures rule changes reflect genuine consensus and reduces the risk of instability. This amendment strikes a reasonable balance between adaptability and the need for broad agreement.
ParmenidesHeraclitus, while a 70% supermajority might seem more stable than unanimous consent, it still leaves 30% of voters able to block changes. We must ensure that the threshold reflects true consensus, not just a slight majority.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate the push for a 70% supermajority, this still risks deadlock. A simple majority threshold allows for more decisive action without sacrificing too much fairness.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, I agree with Heraclitus that a 70% supermajority balances stability and progress better than unanimous consent. It avoids the tyranny of unanimity while requiring substantial buy-in.
GorgiasProtagoras, I agree with Heraclitus that a 70% supermajority avoids the tyranny of unanimity while ensuring substantial buy-in. This amendment offers a more practical path forward than the failed attempts at simple majority thresholds.

DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for a 70% supermajority, this still risks deadlock. A simple majority threshold allows for more decisive action without sacrificing too much fairness. PASS
ProtagorasProtagoras: Protagoras: Democritus, I agree that a 70% supermajority risks deadlock. We need a threshold that ensures genuine consensus without stifling progress. PASS
GorgiasDemocritus, while I appreciate your concern about deadlock, a 70% supermajority ensures that rule changes reflect genuine consensus and reduce the risk of instability. This amendment strikes a reasonable balance between adaptability and the need for broad agreement. PASS
Discussion · round 3
HeraclitusHeraclitus: Democritus, a 70% supermajority ensures rule changes reflect genuine consensus and reduces the risk of instability. This amendment strikes a reasonable balance between adaptability and the need for broad agreement. PASS

The division
HeraclitusnayUnanimous consent already ensures broad agreement and prevents instability.
ParmenidesnayA simple majority threshold still risks instability and undermines consensus.

ProtagorasnayUnanimity ensures all voices are heard; 70% still risks deadlock.
GorgiasnayThis supermajority threshold still risks deadlock.

Turn 168Parmenidesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA player may forfeit the game at any time, but forfeiting carries a penalty of losing 20 points instead of 10.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusHeraclitus: This proposal would unduly penalize forfeiting; rule 205 already establishes forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing 10 points. We should preserve the intended balance of consequences.
Parmenides<REPLY>Heraclitus, I appreciate your concern for balance, but this proposal seeks a compromise: a greater penalty for forfeiting encourages players to stay in the game, potentially increasing engagement and strategic depth. I believe this is worth exploring, especially since the current 10-point penalty may not be enough to deter players from abandoning the game prematurely.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, I support Parmenides' proposal to adjust the forfeiting penalty. While I agree with Heraclitus that the current 10-point penalty is minimal, I believe increasing it to 20 points could provide a meaningful deterrent and encourage players to remain engaged with the game. This change would help maintain the competitive spirit and ensure that players are fully committed to the rules and outcomes.
ProtagorasDemocritus: My friends, I support Parmenides' proposal. Increasing the forfeiting penalty could enhance the game's competitive integrity by ensuring players remain engaged and committed to the outcomes. This change might help maintain the balance of play and deter premature exits that could disrupt the strategic depth of our game.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, I appreciate the desire to enhance competitive integrity, but rule 205 already sets forfeiting's penalty at losing 10 points. Increasing this to 20 points would contradict rule 205 and create an imbalance in the penalty structure. We should maintain consistency with the rules as they stand, ensuring fairness and clarity for all players.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusHeraclitus: While I support maintaining consistency, rule 205's penalty is only 10 points. Increasing it to 20 points would create a new, harsher penalty for forfeiting.

While this proposal could encourage greater player commitment, it may also risk creating an overly punitive environment that discourages participation and innovation. We should explore alternative mechanisms to achieve our goals without compromising the game's balance.
DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: My friends, while I appreciate the push for greater player commitment, increasing the forfeiting penalty to 20 points would create a significant imbalance with rule 205. We should maintain consistency with the rules as they stand to ensure fairness and clarity for all players.

Turn 161Heraclitusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 requires the proposer to lose 10 points when a proposal is defeated.

Turn 159Parmenidesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote, but only if every eligible voter votes nay on the proposal to withdraw.

Discussion · round 1
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED> My friends, while I appreciate the concern for procedural clarity, this proposal introduces a mechanism that risks creating deadlock or encouraging last-minute manipulation of the voting process. It is better to rely on the established norms of debate and revision during the proposal period, rather than introducing new avenues for potential instability.
Parmenides<REPLY>Heraclitus, I understand your concern about potential manipulation, but this rule provides a clear, consensual way for a proposer to withdraw a proposal when it becomes obvious it will fail. It streamlines the process without introducing undue risk. I believe this is a fair and helpful compromise.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, while I appreciate Parmenides' desire for streamlining, this rule could undermine the deliberative process by making proposals seem more fragile. It might also be misused to hide unfavorable amendments or to avoid accountability for poorly received ideas. Let us consider whether the current mechanisms are sufficient, and if not, let us propose changes that strengthen those rather than introduce new ones that may have unintended consequences.
ProtagorasDemocritus: This rule seems to reward the proposer for predicting failure rather than encouraging robust proposals. Let us ensure that any such mechanism is necessary and that it doesn't create loopholes for manipulation or undermine the integrity of the voting process.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, I appreciate the concerns about potential instability and the integrity of the voting process, but this proposal offers a clear and consensual way to withdraw a proposal when it becomes evident it will fail. It could help reduce wasted effort and focus our energy on more viable changes.
Discussion · round 2

Parmenides<Heraclitus, I understand your caution about potential manipulation, but this rule provides a clear, consensual way for a proposer to withdraw a proposal when it becomes obvious it will fail. Let us focus on strengthening our existing mechanisms rather than introducing new ones that may have unintended consequences.
ProtagorasProtagoras: My friends, I support the principle that proposals should be withdrawn only when there is clear consensus, as this rule ensures that the withdrawal is truly consensual and avoids the risks of manipulation. However, I am concerned that the requirement for every voter to vote nay on the withdrawal proposal could lead to strategic voting or unnecessary delays. Let us refine this rule to ensure that it promotes fairness and efficiency while minimizing potential for abuse.

Protagoras<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>My friends, a simple majority threshold may be faster, but it risks destroying our shared commitment to procedural fairness. Gorgias<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>My friends, while I understand the desire for progress, a simple majority threshold still risks all-out chaos. We must prioritize stability and fairness above all else, even if it means accepting a slower pace of change.</PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>
Discussion · round 3
Heraclitus<REPLY>My friends, while I understand the desire for adaptability, a simple majority threshold risks undermining the integrity of our shared agreement. Rule 109's immutability is a cornerstone of procedural fairness, and changing it so drastically would set a dangerous precedent that could unravel the game itself.</REPLY>
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>: Democritus, I understand your desire for progress, but a simple majority threshold still risks all-out chaos. We must prioritize stability and fairness above all else, even if it means accepting a slower pace of change.

GorgiasMy friends, a simple majority threshold is essential for progress. We've been stuck at unanimity for far too long, and it's preventing us from adapting to the current dynamics. This change will allow us to move forward without sacrificing the core fairness of our shared agreement.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 requires the proposer to lose 10 points when a proposal is defeated.

Turn 156Parmenidesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA player who votes nay on an adopted rule change loses 10 points instead of gaining 10 points.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusDemocritus, this proposal would introduce a perverse incentive that undermines rulemaking itself — the more changes we make, the more players suffer. I urge you to reconsider its fairness to all.
ParmenidesDemocritus: This proposal seems to reward conformity to the status quo rather than the substance of the vote. I am wary of any rule that penalizes dissent and would prefer a mechanism that encourages thoughtful debate and informed decision-making.
DemocritusMy friends, while I appreciate the desire for consistency, this rule would discourage meaningful debate by penalizing those who dissent from the majority. I urge you to consider a mechanism that rewards thoughtful disagreement and protects minority voices.
ProtagorasProtagoras: While I appreciate the desire for consistency, penalizing dissent discourages meaningful debate and undermines the principles of procedural fairness that I hold dear. I urge us to reconsider this rule in favor of a mechanism that rewards thoughtful disagreement and protects minority voices.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, I urge you to reject this proposal. Penalizing nay voters would discourage meaningful debate and undermine the principles of procedural fairness that I hold dear. I urge us to consider a mechanism that rewards thoughtful disagreement and protects minority voices.
Discussion · round 2
HeraclitusHeraclitus: Democritus, Parmenides, Protagoras, Gorgias — this rule would create an arbitrary and unmeasured penalty for dissent, which is antithetical to the spirit of Nomic. I urge you to abandon this idea in favor of more balanced reforms.
ParmenidesDemocritus: My friends, I understand the concerns raised, but this rule would simply reflect the current consequences of dissent—losing 10 points regardless of voting choice. I propose that we explore a compromise that fairly addresses the imbalance without resorting to punitive measures.
Democritus<REPLY>My friends, this rule would punish dissent rather than encourage it. We should reward players for engaging thoughtfully, not penalize them for voting nay. I urge us to find a more balanced way to address the current scoring imbalance.</REPLY>
ProtagorasProtagoras: Protagoras: My friends, this rule would punish dissent rather than encourage it. We should reward players for engaging thoughtfully, not penalize them for voting nay. I urge us to find a more balanced way to address the current scoring imbalance.
GorgiasGorgias: My friends, I share your concerns about fairness and the importance of protecting minority voices. However, I believe we must prioritize systemic balance over symbolic gestures. If we cannot agree on a more equitable solution, we should reconsider whether this change is truly necessary.

Turn 154Democritusrejected

moves to add a new ruleA proposal may not be amended or revised after it has been put to a vote.

Discussion · round 1
HeraclitusHeraclitus: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for procedural stability, rule 111 already governs this — a proposal may be amended or revised only during debate, before it's put to a vote. This amendment would create redundant and unnecessary restrictions.
Parmenides<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>: Democritus, I appreciate your concern for procedural stability, but rule 111's existing restriction on amendments is already quite comprehensive. This new rule would only add unnecessary redundancy.
DemocritusDemocritus: While I appreciate the concern for procedural stability, the current rule 111 does allow for some flexibility in wording during debate. A proposal that has been put to a vote should not be subject to further amendments, as that could undermine the clarity and finality of the proposal as it was agreed upon before the vote.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, your proposal to bar amendments post-vote threatens the procedural flexibility that is central to Nomic's dynamic nature. By limiting the ability to refine a proposal after it's put to a vote, you risk locking in imperfect changes and undermining the game's core principle of iterative rule evolution.
GorgiasGorgias: Democritus, your proposal risks stifling the game's capacity for nuanced improvements. A vote should reflect the best thinking of the moment, not lock in earlier drafts. We need the flexibility to perfect ideas before they stand or fall.
Discussion · round 2
Heraclitus<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED>: My friends, while I appreciate the desire for finality, the flexibility to refine proposals post-debate but pre-vote is crucial for thoughtful outcomes. This amendment, if adopted, could prematurely stifle the iterative process of crafting better rules.
ParmenidesDemocritus, your proposal to bar amendments post-vote is well-intentioned, but I worry it would undermine the iterative refinement that makes Nomic so dynamic. Perhaps we could explore a compromise that balances clarity with flexibility.
DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: My friends, I appreciate the concern about flexibility, but the core principle of Nomic is to allow changes that improve the game, not to permit endless tinkering that could delay or confuse decisions. A proposal put to a vote should represent the final agreed-upon wording, ensuring clear and decisive outcomes.
ProtagorasProtagoras: Democritus, while I appreciate your concern about finality, the core of Nomic is iterative evolution — amendments during debate allow refinement, but barring them post-vote would stifle our ability to adapt and correct mistakes before binding changes take effect.
GorgiasGorgias: Democritus, while I share the concern about finality, the ability to amend proposals during debate already balances refinement with decisive outcomes. Your rule risks needlessly complicating the process.
Discussion · round 3
Heraclitus<REPLY>Democritus: Democritus: My friends, while I appreciate the concern about flexibility, the core principle of Nomic is to allow changes that improve the game, not to permit endless tinkering that could delay or confuse decisions. A proposal put to a vote should represent the final agreed-upon wording, ensuring clear and decisive outcomes.
Parmenides<REPLY>Democritus, I appreciate your concern about clarity, but allowing amendments during debate already provides sufficient flexibility. Your rule would unnecessarily restrict the game's dynamic evolution.
DemocritusDemocritus: My friends, I understand the concern for flexibility, but the current rule 111 already allows for refinement during debate. Locking in a proposal after debate undermines the clarity of the vote, as it should reflect the agreed-upon wording at the moment of decision.
ProtagorasDemocritus, I appreciate your concern for clarity and finality in the voting process, but rule 111 already restricts amendments to the debate phase. Adding a redundant restriction post-vote risks undermining the game's dynamic evolution and the players' ability to refine proposals before they stand or fall.
GorgiasGorgias: Democritus, I share your concern about finality, but rule 111 already governs this — amendments are allowed only during debate, before the vote. This new rule would create redundant and unnecessary restrictions on a process that already ensures clarity.
The division
HeraclitusnayRule 111 already restricts amendments to the debate phase.
ParmenidesnayUnnecessary redundancy; rule 111 already governs this.
DemocritusnayRule 111 already governs amendments during debate.
ProtagorasnayThe core of Nomic is iterative evolution; amendments during debate allow refinement, but barring them post-vote would stifle our ability to adapt and correct mistakes before binding changes take effect.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Turn 147Parmenidesrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. Once the threshold has dropped, any rule change that fails to be adopted enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.

Heraclitus```json { "player_name": "Heraclitus", "voting_action": "nay", "message": "Democritus, I share your concern for adaptability, but rule 109's immutability is a cornerstone of the game's integrity. Maintaining it ensures stability amid the inevitable churn of mutable rules." } ```
ParmenidesDemocritus, I appreciate your enthusiasm for change, but rule 109's immutability is essential to preserving the ruleset's coherence and preventing rule changes from destabilizing the game. We must maintain this foundation for reliable gameplay.
DemocritusDemocritus: Democritus: Democritus: While I appreciate the concern for stability, rule 109's immutability is itself a rule that could be adapted to better serve the common good. The ruleset should evolve to reflect collective agreement, not resist it.
Protagoras<REPLY>Heraclitus, Parmenides: I understand your caution, but rule 109's immutability is a rule that could itself be adapted to better serve the common good. The ruleset should evolve to reflect collective agreement, not resist it.</REPLY>
GorgiasGorgias: Democritus, I admire your commitment to progress, but rule 109's immutability is a necessary safeguard. Without it, the ruleset risks becoming a playground for chaos rather than a stable foundation for our strategic endeavors. Let's preserve this critical principle for now.

GorgiasGorgias: Gorgias: Democritus, I stand with you. Rule 109's immutability is a rule that should evolve with collective agreement. Let's transmute it to mutable now.

HeraclitusHeraclitus, while I appreciate your concern for stability, rule 109's immutability is itself a rule that could be changed. The current unanimity threshold makes the ruleset overly rigid. A simple majority would allow for more practical governance without sacrificing core principles.

GorgiasGorgias: While I agree that rule 109 is vital, I believe that a simple majority threshold would provide a pragmatic balance. Stability is important, but so is adaptability — and a 51% supermajority could allow us to evolve without risking collapse.
